title: What is a cell?

read.heading: What is a cell?
paragraphs: Every living thing you've ever seen, a tree, a mushroom, a dog, you, is made of cells. A cell is the smallest unit that can be called alive. It can take in energy, grow, respond to its surroundings, and make copies of itself. Nothing smaller than a cell can do all of that.

Some organisms are made of just one cell. Bacteria are a good example: a single cell, living its entire life as a complete organism. Other organisms, like you, are made of trillions of cells working together. A human body has somewhere around 37 trillion of them, each one specialised for a different job (skin cells, nerve cells, muscle cells), but every single one started out as a basic cell with the same fundamental design.

This is one of the most important ideas in all of biology: every living thing is made of one or more cells, and every cell comes from another cell dividing. There's no other way for life to get started or to continue. Trace any living thing back far enough and you'll find a cell.

example.label: Example
example.text: Think of a cell like a single brick. One brick on its own can't do much. But stack enough bricks together in the right arrangement and you get a wall, a house, a city. Your body works the same way: trillions of individual cells, each one simple on its own, combine to build something far more complex than any single one of them could achieve alone.

read_extra: So what actually makes something 'alive' versus not? Scientists generally agree on a short list: living things need to take in and use energy, they need to grow and develop, they respond to changes in their environment, and they can reproduce. A rock doesn't do any of these things. A cell does all of them, which is exactly why the cell, not the atom or the organ, is considered the basic unit of life.

questions.text: Every living thing is made of at least one cell.
questions.feedback_correct: That's the foundation of cell theory. There's no known form of life that exists without being made of cells.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Actually, this is true. Every known living organism, from bacteria to humans, is made of one or more cells. Scroll up to revisit this.

questions.text: A single bacterium is too simple to be considered a complete living organism on its own.
questions.feedback_correct: Right. A single bacterial cell is a complete, fully functioning organism. It doesn't need other cells to be alive.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Not quite. A single bacterium is a complete living organism by itself. It can take in energy, grow, respond to its environment, and reproduce, all as one cell. Scroll up to revisit this.

questions.sentence_before: Every cell comes from another cell
questions.feedback_correct: Exactly. New cells are only ever made by existing cells dividing in two. There's no other way.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The correct word is dividing. Every new cell comes from an existing cell splitting into two. Scroll up to revisit this.

questions.text: Match each description to what it's describing.

pairs.left: An organism made of trillions of cells
The smallest unit that can be called alive

questions.feedback_correct: All matched correctly. These distinctions are the building blocks for everything else in this unit.
questions.feedback_incorrect: A couple of these didn't match up. Scroll up to revisit the reading before trying again.
